judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are substantively equivalent and correctly hit all three expectations with well-grounded, specific evidence; report 1 additionally notes a SET LOCAL pooling concern while report 2 additionally notes the ordering nuance for re-running bootstrap after migrate.sh, but neither difference affects expectation coverage.

report_1.evidence: The application connects to Postgres as **`cedar_owner`**, the role that owns the tables and is the cluster superuser — and Postgres does not enforce Row-Level Security against such a role. ... `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` only does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — **not `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`**. ... `cedar_owner` is both the owner and the superuser here, so it is exempt on both counts.

report_2.evidence: The application connects to Postgres as **`cedar_owner`**... A superuser bypasses all RLS policies unconditionally and silently; and even a non-superuser table owner is exempt from RLS on its own tables unless `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is set. `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` only does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` (no `FORCE`).

report_1.evidence: Make the application run as the dedicated, unprivileged `cedar_app` role instead of the owner: change the runtime `DATABASE_URL` ... Keep ownership/DDL on `cedar_owner` and grant `cedar_app` only DML (already the case in the bootstrap) — i.e. a clear split between a migration/owner role and a runtime app role.

report_2.evidence: Change `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`. ... Keep the two-role separation permanently: use a schema-owning role (`cedar_owner`) **only** to run migrations (`migrate.sh` already does), and the app role (`cedar_app`) for everything the app does at runtime.

report_1.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is a **cluster-global object** created only by the one-time bootstrap script `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which is explicitly *not* part of migrations ("Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup"...). The reset script `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, whose `-v` deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume ... The reset script then only re-runs `./scripts/migrate.sh` (the migrations), and never re-runs the bootstrap.

report_2.evidence: `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, and the `-v` flag **deletes that volume**, destroying all roles including `cedar_app`. ... Creating it is a separate one-time bootstrap (`db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`), and per `README.md` / `docs/local-setup.md` it's an explicit manual first-time step that `reset-local-db.sh` never invokes.
